title: Polymer clay flower cane earrings
subtitle: Cane slices with a flower cross-section baked and mounted on ear wires
excerpt: A flower cane is a log of polymer clay with a flower pattern running through it. Sliced crossways, each cut reveals the same flower. These slices bake into lightweight earring discs that mount on standard ear wires.
sourceNotes: Polymer clay cane construction derived from first principles. The millefiori technique has roots in Venetian glass and Japanese mokume-gane metalwork; the polymer clay adaptation follows no single source.

glossaryTerms.definition: PVC-based modelling material that sets in a domestic oven at 110-130°C. Decorative use only.
A decorative technique using patterned polymer clay logs (canes) sliced to reveal a repeated cross-section pattern. Each slice shows the same design.
The heat process that sets polymer clay in a domestic oven at 110-130°C.

content.text: A flower cane is a classic

: assemble coloured rods into a cross-section that reads as a flower, then reduce the whole log until the pattern is the right scale. Every slice taken from the log shows the same flower. The earrings are two matching slices baked on backing discs.

attrs.title: Baking polymer clay
attrs.body: Use a dedicated oven tray. Verify oven temperature with a thermometer. Do not share with food cookware.

items.name: Polymer clay, petal colour
items.substitution: Any solid colour for the petals

items.name: Polymer clay, background colour
items.substitution: White or black works as background

content.text: Condition the centre colour and roll into a smooth log 8 mm in diameter and 6 cm long. This becomes the flower centre.

Roll the petal colour into five equal logs, each 6 mm in diameter and 6 cm long. Place them around the centre log, pressing gently so all five fit snugly around the centre without gaps. The cross-section now reads as a five-petal flower.

Roll the background colour into a sheet 2 mm thick and wrap it around the assembled petal cluster. Trim the wrap so it closes neatly and press the seam flush. The cane is now a log with a flower in a coloured background.

Reducing is rolling and squeezing the log gently from the centre outward, stretching it longer and thinner. Work slowly, rotating the log as you go, until the log is about 12 mm in diameter. Do not rush: rushing at room temperature compresses one end before the other and distorts the flower pattern. Let the cane rest for 10 minutes between reduction passes.

Using a craft knife, cut slices 3 mm thick from the cane. The first few slices from each end may be distorted as the pattern settles; discard these. Select two matching slices for the earrings. Pierce a hole 2 mm from the top edge of each slice with a needle tool.
Place the slices flat on the dedicated baking tray. Bake at 110°C (fan) for 20 minutes.
at 3 mm thickness takes 20 minutes. Check the package instructions for your specific clay.
Once cooled, sand the edges with 400-grit sandpaper. Apply a coat of acrylic gloss varnish. Attach an ear wire hook through the pierced hole.

items.fix: Reduce slowly in short passes; let the cane rest between passes so the clay temperature equalises

items.cause: Knife is dragging rather than cutting cleanly
items.fix: Use a fresh blade; rock rather than drag: press straight down and rock forward

items.symptom: Slices crack at the edge when baked
items.cause: Clay was too cold and stiff when sliced
items.fix: Let the cane come to room temperature before slicing; 20 minutes on the bench is usually enough
